      Oh Elon
   =20
 =20

Imagine taking the job! Yesterday Elon Musk  tweeted a poll asking if he sh=
ould step down as =E2=80=9Chead=E2=80=9D of Twitter Inc.; 17.5 million peop=
le voted, and=C2=A0the results were 57.5% to 42.5%=C2=A0in favor of him ste=
pping down. =E2=80=9CI will abide by the results of this poll,=E2=80=9D he =
said. But he also  tweeted =E2=80=9CAs the saying goes, be careful what you=
 wish, as you might get it.=E2=80=9D  And: =E2=80=9CNo one wants the job wh=
o can actually keep Twitter alive. There is no successor.=E2=80=9D  And: =
=E2=80=9CThe question is not finding a CEO, the question is finding a CEO w=
ho can keep Twitter alive.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0And, listing qualifications for th=
e next chief executive officer: =E2=80=9CYou must like pain a lot. One catc=
h: you have to invest your life savings in Twitter and it has been in the f=
ast lane to bankruptcy since May. Still want the job?=E2=80=9D
Meanwhile Musk has been  trying to  raise money for Twitter at $54.20 per s=
hare, the $44 billion valuation at which he bought it; he   said in October=
=C2=A0that he was =E2=80=9Cobviously overpaying.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Since then t=
hings have, uh, not gone well.=C2=A0So the proposition is =E2=80=A6=C2=A0wh=
at? You take all of your money, you give it to Musk=C2=A0to buy equity in h=
is company at a price that you all agree is absurd? And then you get to wor=
k for him, running a sullen and broken Twitter according to his ever-shifti=
ng whims, until he changes his mind and fires you? And when he fires you he=
   denies you severance and dares you to sue, and  accuses you of being a s=
ex criminal?=C2=A0
I am sure that there are Musk fans who=C2=A0do=C2=A0want the job; as I   ha=
ve said, proximity to Elon Musk is what creates value in modern markets, an=
d so throwing away your life savings to work for him might be a good trade.=
 (Also I=E2=80=99m sure he=E2=80=99s kidding about investing your life savi=
ngs in Twitter.) Still, the candidate pool is probably limited to Musk=E2=
=80=99s most ardent yes-men.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CThose who want power are the one=
s who least deserve it,=E2=80=9D Musk also tweeted.
And so one assumes that =E2=80=9Cbe careful what you wish for=E2=80=9D is a=
ddressed to Twitter=E2=80=99s users, who voted for Musk to leave; the impli=
cation would be that whoever replaces Musk as Twitter=E2=80=99s CEO will be=
 even more capricious and unpopular. Instead of Musk making arbitrary decis=
ions to get rid of stuff he doesn=E2=80=99t like, some Musk lieutenant will=
 make arbitrary decisions to get rid of stuff that he thinks Musk=C2=A0migh=
t=C2=A0not like, while giving Musk himself some deniability.
But other readings are possible. Like most people, I assume that (1) Musk t=
weeted this poll because he has already decided to step down as Twitter CEO=
 (since, after all,  he said that a month ago!) and (2) he is planning to s=
tep down as Twitter CEO to spend more time at Tesla Inc. (since, after all,=
 he  said it in a court hearing about how Tesla=E2=80=99s board gave him a =
record-breaking compensation package to retain his services, and then he we=
nt flying off to run Twitter instead). One possibility is that =E2=80=9Cbe =
careful what you wish for=E2=80=9D is directed at=C2=A0Tesla=E2=80=99s=C2=
=A0board or shareholders. =E2=80=9CSure now you say that you want me back r=
unning Tesla full time, but look at the mess I=E2=80=99m making here, are y=
ou sure you want that at Tesla too?=E2=80=9D Musk owns companies that (1) p=
roduce self-driving=C2=A0cars, (2) send rockets into space, (3) make human =
brain implants and (4) run a website where people make fun of him. If he is=
 having some sort of self-sabotaging meltdown, it is much better for him to=
 spend his time on the making-fun-of-him website than the brain implants or=
 the cars or the rockets. If he messes up the brain implants, people die! I=
f he messes up the website where people make fun of him, they will make fun=
 of him more. If you use Twitter as a source of comedy and a way to get in =
fights, then it is=C2=A0good=C2=A0for Twitter to be run badly. Rockets don=
=E2=80=99t work that way.
Or=C2=A0of course =E2=80=9Cbe careful what you wish for=E2=80=9D could be a=
 reminder to Musk himself. He wanted to buy Twitter, and oh boy did he ever=
 end up buying Twitter, whoops. Ten million years ago, in April, when Musk =
first announced that he had taken a stake in Twitter,   I wrote:
Look this all makes complete sense,=C2=A0obvious, intuitive, simple sense. =
If you are the richest person in the world, and annoying, and you constantl=
y play a computer game, and you get a lot of enjoyment and a sense of ident=
ity from that game and are maybe a little addicted, then at some point you =
might=C2=A0have some suggestions for improvements in the game. So you might=
 leave comments and email the company that makes the game saying =E2=80=9Ch=
ey you should try my ideas.=E2=80=9D And the company=C2=A0might ignore you =
(or respond politely but not move fast enough for your liking). It might oc=
cur to you: =E2=80=9CLook, I am the richest person in the world; how much c=
ould this game company possibly cost? I should just buy it and change the g=
ame however I want.=E2=80=9D Even if your complaints are quite minor, why s=
houldn=E2=80=99t you get to play=C2=A0exactly=C2=A0the game you want? Even =
if you have=C2=A0no=C2=A0complaints, why not=C2=A0own=C2=A0the game you lov=
e, just to make sure it continues to be exactly what you want?=C2=A0
And that is more or less what has happened: He bought Twitter, brought back=
 accounts whose politics aligned with his,   banned journalists who critici=
zed him, banned mentions of other social media platforms, and generally tai=
lored the Twitter experience to what he thought he wanted.
And yet! From the outside, it does not seem like he is having fun. It turns=
 out that very little of the experience of Twitter is about, like, the soft=
ware; the edit button does not make anyone happy. The experience of Twitter=
 is about the people, and in the course of running Twitter Musk has made a =
lot of people mad at him. (In  what might be a first in internet message bo=
ard moderating, Musk=E2=80=99s mom has been=C2=A0tweeting to  defend his mo=
deration decisions.) Twitter is about comedy and getting in fights, but it =
is also about connection, about finding an audience of people who understan=
d and appreciate you. The basic problem is that, if you run a message board=
, you can tweak the buttons, and you can ban users, but you can=E2=80=99t m=

I want to make sort of a generic point about crypto shadow banks. Let=E2=80=
=99s say you have a crypto shadow bank, say a lending platform that takes c=
ustomer deposits and uses them to lend to crypto hedge funds. It has custom=
er deposits of $1 billion, it has shareholders=E2=80=99 equity of $100 mill=
ion, and it buys $1.1 billion of assets, meaning mostly cryptocurrencies an=
d loans to crypto hedge funds.=C2=A0The value of the assets goes down (to $=
1 billion, or maybe a bit more, or maybe a lot less), there=E2=80=99s a run=
 on the bank, and the lending platform halts withdrawals and files for bank=
ruptcy.
It goes to bankruptcy court and says =E2=80=9Chi, we owe customers $1 billi=
on and we have assets that might be worth $1 billion or might not, but we=
=E2=80=99re gonna try to get as much as we can for them.=E2=80=9D There are=
 two possible paths here.
One is that the platform can sell off the assets for cash. It had some Bitc=
oins that it sells on an exchange; it has some stablecoins that it converts=
 to dollars; it has some loans to hedge funds that it tries to collect or s=
ell to other investors. Maybe it finds a buyer for all the assets; maybe it=
 sells them off piecemeal. The buyers get the assets and pay cash. The buye=
rs do not get any liabilities; the liabilities stay with the platform, in b=
ankruptcy court. At the end of the process, the platform has some cash, and=
 it gives that to its customers. If it ends up with $1 billion (or more) in=
 cash, after paying expenses, then the customers get all their money back. =
If it ends up with $900 million in cash, the customers get 90 cents on the =
dollar. In any case, they get a check from the bankruptcy estate in the ban=
kruptcy process.
The other possible path is that the platform can sell itself as a going con=
cern, or going-ish anyway. It finds a buyer that is itself a crypto shadow =
bank =E2=80=94 let=E2=80=99s say a big well-known crypto exchange =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0and the buyer says =E2=80=9Csure we will take over your customer liab=
ilities.=E2=80=9D The buyer buys (1) the platform=E2=80=99s assets (its Bit=
coins, loans, etc.) and (2) its customer list; it also acquires the platfor=
m=E2=80=99s customer liabilities.=C2=A0It does not pay cash, or not very mu=
ch: It pays for the assets by taking on the liabilities. (If the assets are=
 worth more than the liabilities =E2=80=94=C2=A0or if the customer list is =
very valuable =E2=80=94=C2=A0then maybe it kicks in some cash to pay the ba=
nkruptcy advisers and maybe, even, the platform=E2=80=99s shareholders, but=
 don=E2=80=99t count on that.) No cash flows through the bankruptcy estate;=
 the customers don=E2=80=99t get checks.
Instead, the customers wake up one day to find that they are now customers =
of the buyer. This is an improvement in their situation. Before, they were =
customers of a bankrupt crypto lending platform that had frozen withdrawals=
. Now, they are customers of a big solvent crypto exchange that processes w=
ithdrawals normally. They can withdraw their money! Their accounts are all =
there! They can get 100 cents on the dollar!
Perhaps they all will: =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99ve had enough of this,=E2=80=9D t=
hey=E2=80=99ll say, and take their cash out. In that scenario, the buyer wi=
ll have to pay out $1 billion in cash to customers in short order, and will=
 be left with the platform=E2=80=99s=C2=A0assets, its crypto or loans or wh=
atever.
  [1]
=C2=A0If the assets were worth $1.1 billion, that=E2=80=99s good for the bu=
yer, and that=E2=80=99s a real possibility: If the buyer solved a liquidity=
 crisis for the seller, then the assets might quickly bounce back. If the a=
ssets were worth $900 million, the buyer loses money, and that is also a re=
al possibility: When crypto shadow banks blow up they tend to blow up prett=
y hard. In any case, in this scenario the buyer has not gotten much value o=
ut of the seller=E2=80=99s customer list: All the customers quit immediatel=
y.
But this is not, I think, the most likely outcome, or the one the buyer is =
betting on. More realistically, most of the customers will think =E2=80=9Ca=
h, well, I got burned by trusting that old insolvent crypto shadow bank, bu=
t now I am a customer of a well-capitalized industry-leading crypto exchang=
e, and surely my money is safe. Also my new exchange has such a good user i=
nterface and a wide range of exciting trading and lending opportunities; I =
am going to take advantage of them.=E2=80=9D So the customers will keep the=
ir cash on the platform. The buyer =E2=80=94=C2=A0the exchange that now has=
 them as customers =E2=80=94=C2=A0does not have to pay out $1 billion in ca=
sh. Perhaps it attracts=C2=A0more=C2=A0cash, as grateful and confident cust=
omers experience its good trading interface. They trade, they make or lose =
money, they pay fees, life goes on.
In this scenario you can say things like =E2=80=9Cthe rescuing exchange bou=
ght the bankrupt platform=E2=80=99s assets for $1 billion,=E2=80=9D and you=
=E2=80=99re not exactly wrong. But the rescuing exchange didn=E2=80=99t pay=
 out $1 billion in cash for the bankrupt platform=E2=80=99s assortment of a=
ssets. It did something else. It got the assets and=C2=A0guaranteed=C2=A0th=
e bankrupt platform=E2=80=99s liabilities.
There=E2=80=99s nothing particularly =E2=80=9Ccrypto=E2=80=9D about any of =
this, I should say, and rescues of failing actual banks tend to work much t=
he same way (though they tend to be orchestrated by bank regulators and bac=
kstopped by deposit insurance). If your bank fails, it would be very unusua=
l for you to end up with a sack of cash and a note reading =E2=80=9Cfind an=
other bank.=E2=80=9D Far, far, far more likely, you will just automatically=
 end up with an account at a different bank, and it won=E2=80=99t even occu=
r to you to withdraw your money. Your bank failed and now you have a better=
 bank, the system worked, it=E2=80=99s fine.
The bankrupt platform   may or may not have had a solvency crisis =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0the assets might or might not have been worth less than the liabiliti=
es =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it certainly had a liquidity crisis; customers wanted=
 their money back and there wasn=E2=80=99t enough ready money for them. The=
 exchange solved the liquidity crisis, not necessarily by coming up with re=
ady money, but by=C2=A0slowing the withdrawals. (By promising to have ready=
 money.)=C2=A0The exchange can say =E2=80=9Ceverything is fine now, we have=
 your money,=E2=80=9D and customers will breathe a sigh of relief and stop =
withdrawing money.
Now, in the general case, the bankrupt crypto lending platform was saying e=
xactly that =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Ceverything is fine, we have your money=
=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0minutes before freezing withdrawals and filing for=
 bankruptcy. The thing that has changed is that people didn=E2=80=99t belie=
ve the bankrupt crypto platform, so they kept withdrawing money and it coll=
apsed; they believe the big exchange that took it over, so they stop.
If they=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0stop =E2=80=94=C2=A0if this doesn=E2=80=99t=
 work =E2=80=94=C2=A0then hopefully the big exchange has the money to pay t=
hem out! But the big exchange won=E2=80=99t, like,=C2=A0put all that money =
in an escrow account just to win a bankruptcy auction. The big exchange wil=
l go to the bankrupt platform=E2=80=99s executives and bankruptcy advisers,=
 and the bankruptcy court, and say =E2=80=9Ctrust us, we=E2=80=99re good fo=
r the money, we=E2=80=99re a big exchange.=E2=80=9D Hopefully they will do =
some due diligence on the exchange and satisfy themselves that that=E2=80=
=99s true. But, in crypto, things change fast, and it=E2=80=99s weirdly har=
d to get audited financial statements.
The stuff I wrote above more or less describes, at a high level, FTX=E2=80=
=99s bailouts of   Voyager Digital Ltd. and   BlockFi Inc.=C2=A0this summer=
.=C2=A0In each case, a lending platform ran into liquidity trouble,=C2=A0an=
d FTX and its affiliated trading firm Alameda Research agreed to take over =
its customer accounts, paying not-very-many actual dollars, acquiring the a=
ssets and promising to cash out customers who wanted out.
  [2]
=C2=A0(It did not actually close either acquisition.) This worked out very =
poorly, in the sense that FTX and Alameda themselves turned out not to be p=
articularly solvent and went bankrupt last month,=C2=A0so their guarantee o=
f the customers wasn=E2=80=99t worth much.
Last month   I speculated a little bit about why FTX and Alameda =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0which over the summer were running into fatal trouble themselves =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0might have devoted so much of their scarce money to bailing out=
 other insolvent crypto shadow banks. I hope that the explanation above hel=
ps make the answer clear? One of my proposed explanations last month was:
Crypto prices, and firms, crashed earlier this year, and FTX/Alameda spotte=
d an=C2=A0opportunity to acquire new customer deposits cheaply and use them=
 for nefarious purposes. Like, you pay zero dollars for the equity of some =
busted crypto lending platform, you roll the customers over to become FTX c=
ustomers, you cash out anyone who wants to cash out, you assume that most p=
eople will trust FTX (their savior) and=C2=A0not=C2=A0cash out, and then yo=
u use=C2=A0their=C2=A0deposits to fund your wild speculations. If FTX/Alame=
da were=C2=A0already=C2=A0using customer deposits for bad reasons, and losi=
ng them, then acquiring more customer deposits would be a way to keep thing=
s going.

If you are a public accounting firm, you are in the business of signing you=
r name to a piece of paper saying =E2=80=9Cthis company=E2=80=99s financial=
 statements are basically true,=E2=80=9D and charging for it. Before signin=
g your name, you will do your best to confirm that the financial statements=
 are true, but there is always a risk that you=E2=80=99re wrong. If you are=
 wrong you might get sued or fined; also, though, if you are wrong too ofte=
n your signature will lose its value and people will stop paying you for it=
. Your high-level goal is to (1) minimize that risk while (2) maximizing yo=
ur revenue. Broadly speaking, you minimize risk by being conservative, by o=
nly auditing the statements of simple trustworthy companies in stable indus=
tries, and you maximize revenue by being aggressive, by auditing the statem=
ents of companies in fast-growing industries and by being, uh, customer-fri=
endly about your audits.
The tensions here are all very obvious and hard, but sometimes there are ea=
sy calls. Like if you got into aggressively auditing statements of companie=
s in a complicated and risky industry because you were betting on its rapid=
 growth, and then people lose confidence in the finances of companies in th=
at industry and it starts shrinking, then (1) the risk of auditing that ind=
ustry has gone way up and (2) the potential revenue has gone way down. Why =
bother?   Anyway:

Mazars Group, the accounting firm used by crypto giant Binance Holdings Ltd=
. and other big players in the industry to vouch for their assets held in r=
eserve, has halted all such work for crypto clients, dealing a blow to an i=
ndustry seeking to shore up confidence in the wake of FTX=E2=80=99s collaps=
e.=C2=A0
In an email sent by the French firm, Mazars said it had suspended work for =
cryptocurrency firms because of indications that markets haven=E2=80=99t be=
en reassured by the =E2=80=9Cproof-of-reserves=E2=80=9D reports it had publ=
ished so far. The firm was also concerned about intense media scrutiny, the=
 email said. A Mazars spokesperson later said the suspension was limited to=
 its provision of proof-of-reserves reports, citing =E2=80=9Cconcerns regar=
ding the way these reports are understood by the public.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6
Proof-of-reserves reports have faced scrutiny as they are not comparable to=
 a full audit, in that they only show a firm=E2=80=99s assets, not its liab=
ilites, and instead serve as snapshots in time that say information provide=
d by clients broadly checks out. =E2=80=A6
=E2=80=9CMany audit firms are scared to work with crypto businesses,=E2=80=
=9D said Binance CEO Changpeng =E2=80=9CCZ=E2=80=9D Zhao in a Thursday inte=
rview on CNBC. When asked why Binance hasn=E2=80=99t engaged a Big Four aud=
itor =E2=80=94 a moniker that refers to the largest accountancy companies P=
wC, Deloitte, EY and KPMG =E2=80=94 Zhao added that such firms =E2=80=9Cdon=
=E2=80=99t even know how to audit crypto exchanges.=E2=80=9D

In theory, auditing is especially valuable in an industry where some big fi=
rms are giant scams and some are not and it is hard to tell the difference =
from the outside. In practice, it is not clear that auditors can tell the d=
ifference either, and the risk is high.

  [2] BlockFi got an FTX rescue *without* filing for bankruptcy: It agreed =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Imagine taking the job! Yesterday Elon Musk <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0=
cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9lbG9ubXVzay9zdGF0dXMvMTYwNDYxNzY0Mzk3MzEyNDA5Nw/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cB07f883d9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> tweeted a poll</a> asking if he sho=
uld step down as =E2=80=9Chead=E2=80=9D of Twitter Inc.; 17.5 million peopl=
e voted, and=C2=A0the results were 57.5% to 42.5%=C2=A0in favor of him step=
ping down. =E2=80=9CI will abide by the results of this poll,=E2=80=9D he s=
aid. But he also <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3=
0025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9lbG9ubXVzay9zdGF0dXMvMTYwNDYyMzQy=
NDE2NDI4MjM2OA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBa83bb263" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" it=
emscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decor=
ation: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000=
000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> tweeted</a> =E2=
=80=9CAs the saying goes, be careful what you wish, as you might get it.=E2=
=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.28=
7534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9lbG9ubXVzay9zdGF0dXMvMTYwNDYyODc2MTM5NTEzOD=
U2MQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB9c5ae359" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> And</a>: =E2=80=9CNo one w=
ants the job who can actually keep Twitter alive. There is no successor.=E2=
=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.28=
7534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9lbG9ubXVzay9zdGF0dXMvMTYwNDYyMTEwMTI0NTQxOT=
UyMA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB09192289" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> And</a>: =E2=80=9CThe ques=
tion is not finding a CEO, the question is finding a CEO who can keep Twitt=
er alive.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9lbG9ubXVzay9zdGF0dXMvMTYwN=
DYyNjEwMzMyNjI1MzA1Ng/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2be9d1f2" itemprop=3D"StoryL=
ink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">And</a>, l=
isting qualifications for the next chief executive officer: =E2=80=9CYou mu=
st like pain a lot. One catch: you have to invest your life savings in Twit=
ter and it has been in the fast lane to bankruptcy since May. Still want th=
e job?=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile Musk has been <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VtYWZv=
ci5jb20vYXJ0aWNsZS8xMi8xNi8yMDIyL2Vsb24tbXVza3MtdGVhbS1pcy1zZWVraW5nLW5ldy1=
pbnZlc3RvcnMtZm9yLXR3aXR0ZXI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3b4ebbe6" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> tr=
ying</a> to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/300254=
50.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvYmIwNDdjOGYtZjk3ZC00ZTNhLThiY=
mItNTBkMDQ5NGM4YzQ4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB563284ea" itemprop=3D"StoryLin=
k" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-=
decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color:=
 #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> raise money=
</a> for Twitter at $54.20 per share, the $44 billion valuation at which he=
 bought it; he <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/300=
25450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMT=
AtMTkvZWxvbi1tdXNrLXNheXMtaGUtcy1vYnZpb3VzbHktb3ZlcnBheWluZy1mb3ItdHdpdHRlc=
j9jbXBpZD1CQkQxMjE5MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9=
bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjEyMTkmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07c=
eef4b8524a319a23cB152fd92f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" =
target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important=
; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-sty=
le: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  said in October</a>=C2=A0that he was =
=E2=80=9Cobviously overpaying.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Since then things have, uh, no=
t gone well.=C2=A0So the proposition is =E2=80=A6=C2=A0what? You take all o=
f your money, you <em>give it to Musk=C2=A0</em>to buy equity in his compan=
y at a price that you all agree is absurd? And then you get to work for him=
, running a sullen and broken Twitter according to his ever-shifting whims,=
 until he changes his mind and fires you? And when he fires you he <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTAtMzEvZWxvbi1tdXNr=
LWlzLWJ1c3ktd2l0aC10d2l0dGVyP2NtcGlkPUJCRDEyMTkyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWR=
pdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMTIxOSZ1dG1fY2FtcG=
FpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfe7a2535" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  denies yo=
u severance and dares you to sue</a>, and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud2FzaGluZ3RvbnBvc3Q=
uY29tL3RlY2hub2xvZ3kvMjAyMi8xMi8xMi9tdXNrLWNoaWxkLXBvcm4tcWFub24v/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cB5d7a3f23" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" ta=
rget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; =
border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style=
: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> accuses you of being a sex criminal</a>?=
=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am sure that there are Musk fans who=C2=A0<e=
m>do=C2=A0</em>want the job; as I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbus=
iness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9=
uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDItMTAvZWxvbi1tdXNrLXR3ZWV0cy1ncmVhdC1uZXdzLWZvci1iaX=
Rjb2luLWJhZC1uZXdzLWZvci1lY29uLW1ham9ycz9jbXBpZD1CQkQxMjE5MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGR=
iZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjEyMTkm=
dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe173442f" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
  have said</a>, proximity to Elon Musk is what creates value in modern mar=
kets, and so throwing away your life savings to work for him might be a goo=
d trade. (Also I=E2=80=99m sure he=E2=80=99s kidding about investing your l=
ife savings in Twitter.) Still, the candidate pool is probably limited to M=
usk=E2=80=99s most ardent yes-men.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CThose who want power are t=
he ones who least deserve it,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9lbG9ubXVzay=
9zdGF0dXMvMTYwNDY1MDAyODk5OTQwNTU2OA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1b9c4f39" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">Musk also tweeted</a>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so one assumes that =E2=80=9Cbe careful wh=
at you wish for=E2=80=9D is addressed to Twitter=E2=80=99s <em>users</em>, =
who voted for Musk to leave; the implication would be that whoever replaces=
 Musk as Twitter=E2=80=99s CEO will be even more capricious and unpopular. =
Instead of Musk making arbitrary decisions to get rid of stuff he doesn=E2=
=80=99t like, some Musk lieutenant will make arbitrary decisions to get rid=
 of stuff that he thinks Musk=C2=A0<em>might=C2=A0</em>not like, while givi=
ng Musk himself some deniability.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But other readings are possible. Like most peo=
ple, I assume that (1) Musk tweeted this poll because he has already decide=
d to step down as Twitter CEO (since, after all, <a href=3D"https://link.ma=
il.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYXhpb3MuY29t=

ce, after all, he <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=

oard gave him a record-breaking compensation package to retain his services=
, and then he went flying off to run Twitter instead). One possibility is t=
hat =E2=80=9Cbe careful what you wish for=E2=80=9D is directed at=C2=A0<em>=
Tesla=E2=80=99s</em>=C2=A0board or shareholders. =E2=80=9CSure now you say =
that you want me back running Tesla full time, but look at the mess I=E2=80=
=99m making here, are you sure you want that at Tesla too?=E2=80=9D Musk ow=
ns companies that (1) produce self-driving=C2=A0cars, (2) send rockets into=

ke fun of him. If he is having some sort of self-sabotaging meltdown, it is=

n the brain implants or the cars or the rockets. If he messes up the brain =

him, they will make fun of him more. If you use Twitter as a source of come=
dy and a way to get in fights, then it is=C2=A0<em>good=C2=A0</em>for Twitt=
er to be run badly. Rockets don=E2=80=99t work that way.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or=C2=A0of course =E2=80=9Cbe careful what you=
 wish for=E2=80=9D could be a reminder to Musk himself. He wanted to buy Tw=
itter, and oh boy did he ever end up buying Twitter, whoops. Ten million ye=
ars ago, in April, when Musk first announced that he had taken a stake in T=
witter, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.2=
87534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDQtM=
DQvZWxvbi1tdXNrLWJvdWdodC1zb21lLXR3aXR0ZXI_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTIxOTIyX01PTkVZU1RV=
RkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIxMjE=
5JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB56917efc" itempr=
op=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: =
#000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;=
">  I wrote</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Look this all makes complete sense=
,=C2=A0obvious, intuitive, simple sense. If you are the richest person in t=
he world, and annoying, and you constantly play a computer game, and you ge=
t a lot of enjoyment and a sense of identity from that game and are maybe a=
 little addicted, then at some point you might=C2=A0have some suggestions f=
or improvements in the game. So you might leave comments and email the comp=
any that makes the game saying =E2=80=9Chey you should try my ideas.=E2=80=
=9D And the company=C2=A0might ignore you (or respond politely but not move=
 fast enough for your liking). It might occur to you: =E2=80=9CLook, I am t=
he richest person in the world; how much could this game company possibly c=
ost? I should just buy it and change the game however I want.=E2=80=9D Even=
 if your complaints are quite minor, why shouldn=E2=80=99t you get to play=
=C2=A0<em>exactly=C2=A0</em>the game you want? Even if you have=C2=A0<em>no=
=C2=A0</em>complaints, why not=C2=A0<em>own</em>=C2=A0the game you love, ju=
st to make sure it continues to be exactly what you want?=C2=A0</p></blockq=
uote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And that is more or less what has happened: He=
 bought Twitter, brought back accounts whose politics aligned with his, <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0c=
HM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTItMTkvbXVzay1zLXZv=
bGF0aWxpdHktaXMtYWxpZW5hdGluZy10d2l0dGVyLXMtdG9wLWNvbnRlbnQtY3JlYXRvcnM_Y21=
waWQ9QkJEMTIxOTIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3=
NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIxMjE5JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b=
8524a319a23cB5215b22f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targe=
t=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: s=
olid; background-color: #ccc;">  banned journalists</a> who criticized him,=
 banned mentions of other social media platforms, and generally tailored th=
e Twitter experience to what he thought he wanted.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And yet! From the outside, it does not seem li=
ke he is having fun. It turns out that very little of the experience of Twi=
tter is about, like, the software; the edit button does not make anyone hap=
py. The experience of Twitter is about the people, and in the course of run=
ning Twitter Musk has made a lot of people mad at him. (In <a href=3D"https=
://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dG=
VyLmNvbS9ueWNzb3V0aHBhdy9zdGF0dXMvMTYwNDU4NjEwNDM1NDYzNTc4MA/607f07ceef4b85=
24a319a23cB6d57f384" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> what might be a first</a> in internet messag=
e board moderating, Musk=E2=80=99s mom has been=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNv=
bS9tYXllbXVzay9zdGF0dXMvMTYwMzc1ODc2ODM3ODAyMzkzNw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23c=
B7dfea548" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgr=
ound-color: #ccc;">tweeting</a> to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbu=
siness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9tYXllbXVzay9zdG=
F0dXMvMTYwNDU1MDQ1MjQ0NzY5MDc1Mg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2dd9a4db" itempro=
p=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #=
000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-=
bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"=
> defend</a> his moderation decisions.) Twitter is about comedy and getting=
 in fights, but it is also about connection, about finding an audience of p=
eople who understand and appreciate you. The basic problem is that, if you =
run a message board, you can tweak the buttons, and you can ban users, but =
you can=E2=80=99t make the users who are left love you. And for $44 billion=
 you want love.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudGhlaW5mb3JtYXRpb24uY2=
9tL2FydGljbGVzL2J5LWRheS1lbG9uLW11c2stcnVsZXMtdHdpdHRlci1ieS1uaWdodC1oZXMta=
W52YWRpbmctb3VyLWRyZWFtcz9yYz0xYmRpNWg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB31f24a65" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;"> in dreams</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">According to psychologists who specialize in t=
reating people suffering from nightmares and other sleep disorders, nightly=
 visitations from Elon Musk are happening a lot these days. Such shared dre=
am occurrences, which often happen during periods of instability, offer a b=
roader insight into our collective psyche. =E2=80=9CWhen something is in th=
e press, it triggers a lot of [dream] images,=E2=80=9D said Gayle Delaney, =
a Florida-based clinical psychologist and dream analyst. =E2=80=9CA lot of =
people are thinking about Elon Musk. He demonstrates the human lust for pow=
er, play and creativity=E2=80=A6and he=E2=80=99s also disappointed a lot of=
 people.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A search through Twitter confirms that Musk is=
 turning up constantly in people=E2=80=99s subconscious right now, just as =
he is dominating conversation on the platform itself.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I guess that=E2=80=99s worth paying $44 billio=
n for.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">About that equity sale. <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VtYWZv=
ci5jb20vYXJ0aWNsZS8xMi8xNi8yMDIyL2Vsb24tbXVza3MtdGVhbS1pcy1zZWVraW5nLW5ldy1=
pbnZlc3RvcnMtZm9yLXR3aXR0ZXI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC3b4ebbe6" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Se=
mafor reported</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Musk=E2=80=99s money manager, Jared Birchall, =
reached out to potential investors [last] week, offering shares of Twitter =
at the same price, $54.20, that Musk paid to take the company private in Oc=
tober, the people said.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9COver recent weeks we=E2=80=99ve recei=
ved numerous inbound requests to invest in Twitter,=E2=80=9D it read, accor=
ding to a copy reviewed by Semafor. =E2=80=9CAccordingly, we are pleased to=
 announce a follow-on equity offering for common shares at the original pri=
ce and terms, targeting a year-end close.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ross Gerber, a Tesla investor who said he put =
less than $1 million in Musk=E2=80=99s original takeover of Twitter, confir=
med that he was contacted Thursday evening about another funding round at t=
he $44 billion valuation.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Gerber said he=E2=80=99s considering it but wa=
nts to get a clearer idea of what the plan is. =E2=80=9COne could argue he =
has created value or destroyed value at Twitter. It=E2=80=99s hard to tell =
at this point,=E2=80=9D he said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvYmIwNDdj=
OGYtZjk3ZC00ZTNhLThiYmItNTBkMDQ5NGM4YzQ4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC563284ea"=
 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color=
: #ccc;"> Financial Times adds</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The note did not specify how much Twitter expe=
cted to raise in the new fundraising effort, but said it aimed to close the=
 fundraising by the end of the year.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CEverything has been done haphazardly =
and roughly,=E2=80=9D said investment adviser Ross Gerber, who invested in =
the Twitter deal in October and confirmed he had received the latest offer.=
 =E2=80=9CThey=E2=80=99re doing it because they=E2=80=99re out of money. I =
don=E2=80=99t think [Musk] expected such a big drop in revenue.=E2=80=9D</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A second person whose firm received the offer =
said Musk had indicated the new capital would be used to fund an expansion =
of its business, including a =E2=80=9Chiring spree=E2=80=9D of programmers =
to build a =E2=80=9Csuper app=E2=80=9D that could process payments, among o=
ther services.</p>
</blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also my initial assumption was that, if Musk w=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the other hand, one thing that Musk has alw=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I want to make sort of a generic point about c=
rypto shadow banks. Let=E2=80=99s say you have a crypto shadow bank, say a =
lending platform that takes customer deposits and uses them to lend to cryp=
to hedge funds. It has customer deposits of $1 billion, it has shareholders=
=E2=80=99 equity of $100 million, and it buys $1.1 billion of assets, meani=
ng mostly cryptocurrencies and loans to crypto hedge funds.=C2=A0The value =
of the assets goes down (to $1 billion, or maybe a bit more, or maybe a lot=
 less), there=E2=80=99s a run on the bank, and the lending platform halts w=
ithdrawals and files for bankruptcy.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It goes to bankruptcy court and says =E2=80=9C=
hi, we owe customers $1 billion and we have assets that might be worth $1 b=
illion or might not, but we=E2=80=99re gonna try to get as much as we can f=
or them.=E2=80=9D There are two possible paths here.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One is that the platform can sell off the asse=
ts for cash. It had some Bitcoins that it sells on an exchange; it has some=
 stablecoins that it converts to dollars; it has some loans to hedge funds =
that it tries to collect or sell to other investors. Maybe it finds a buyer=
 for all the assets; maybe it sells them off piecemeal. The buyers get the =
assets and pay cash. The buyers do not get any <em>liabilities</em>; the li=
abilities stay with the platform, in bankruptcy court. At the end of the pr=
ocess, the platform has some cash, and it gives that to its customers. If i=
t ends up with $1 billion (or more) in cash, after paying expenses, then th=
e customers get all their money back. If it ends up with $900 million in ca=
sh, the customers get 90 cents on the dollar. In any case, they get a check=
 from the bankruptcy estate in the bankruptcy process.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The other possible path is that the platform c=
an sell itself as a going concern, or going-ish anyway. It finds a buyer th=
at is itself a crypto shadow bank =E2=80=94 let=E2=80=99s say a big well-kn=
own crypto exchange =E2=80=94=C2=A0and the buyer says =E2=80=9Csure we will=
 take over your customer liabilities.=E2=80=9D The buyer buys (1) the platf=
orm=E2=80=99s assets (its Bitcoins, loans, etc.) and (2) its customer list;=
 it also acquires the platform=E2=80=99s customer liabilities.=C2=A0<em>It =
does not pay cash</em>, or not very much: It pays for the assets by taking =
on the liabilities. (If the assets are worth more than the liabilities =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0or if the customer list is very valuable =E2=80=94=C2=A0then ma=
ybe it kicks in some cash to pay the bankruptcy advisers and maybe, even, t=
he platform=E2=80=99s shareholders, but don=E2=80=99t count on that.) No ca=
sh flows through the bankruptcy estate; the customers don=E2=80=99t get che=
cks.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Instead, the customers wake up one day to find=
 that they are now customers of the buyer. This is an improvement in their =
situation. Before, they were customers of a bankrupt crypto lending platfor=
m that had frozen withdrawals. Now, they are customers of a big solvent cry=
pto exchange that processes withdrawals normally. They can withdraw their m=
oney! Their accounts are all there! They can get 100 cents on the dollar!</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Perhaps they all will: =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99ve h=
ad enough of this,=E2=80=9D they=E2=80=99ll say, and take their cash out. I=
n that scenario, the buyer will have to pay out $1 billion in cash to custo=
mers in short order, and will be left with the platform=E2=80=99s=C2=A0asse=
ts, its crypto or loans or whatever.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#fo=
otnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border=
-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none=
 !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-bloc=
k;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0If the assets were worth $1.1 billion, that=E2=80=99s good for th=
e buyer, and that=E2=80=99s a real possibility: If the buyer solved a liqui=
dity crisis for the seller, then the assets might quickly bounce back. If t=
he assets were worth $900 million, the buyer loses money, and that is also =
a real possibility: When crypto shadow banks blow up they tend to blow up p=
retty hard. In any case, in this scenario the buyer has not gotten much val=
ue out of the seller=E2=80=99s customer list: All the customers quit immedi=
ately.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But this is not, I think, the most likely outc=
ome, or the one the buyer is betting on. More realistically, most of the cu=
stomers will think =E2=80=9Cah, well, I got burned by trusting that old ins=
olvent crypto shadow bank, but now I am a customer of a well-capitalized in=
dustry-leading crypto exchange, and surely my money is safe. Also my new ex=
change has such a good user interface and a wide range of exciting trading =
and lending opportunities; I am going to take advantage of them.=E2=80=9D S=
o the customers will keep their cash on the platform. The buyer =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0the exchange that now has them as customers =E2=80=94=C2=A0does not h=
ave to pay out $1 billion in cash. Perhaps it attracts=C2=A0<em>more=C2=A0<=
/em>cash, as grateful and confident customers experience its good trading i=
nterface. They trade, they make or lose money, they pay fees, life goes on.=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In this scenario you can say things like =E2=
=80=9Cthe rescuing exchange bought the bankrupt platform=E2=80=99s assets f=
or $1 billion,=E2=80=9D and you=E2=80=99re not exactly wrong. But the rescu=
ing exchange didn=E2=80=99t pay out $1 billion in cash for the bankrupt pla=
tform=E2=80=99s assortment of assets. It did something else. It got the ass=
ets and=C2=A0<em>guaranteed=C2=A0</em>the bankrupt platform=E2=80=99s liabi=
lities.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There=E2=80=99s nothing particularly =E2=80=9C=
crypto=E2=80=9D about any of this, I should say, and rescues of failing act=
ual banks tend to work much the same way (though they tend to be orchestrat=
ed by bank regulators and backstopped by deposit insurance). If your bank f=
ails, it would be very unusual for you to end up with a sack of cash and a =
note reading =E2=80=9Cfind another bank.=E2=80=9D Far, far, far more likely=
, you will just automatically end up with an account at a different bank, a=
nd it won=E2=80=99t even occur to you to withdraw your money. Your bank fai=
led and now you have a better bank, the system worked, it=E2=80=99s fine.</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The bankrupt platform <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZX=
JnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTEtMTAvZnR4LWlzLXN0aWxsLWxvb2tpbmctZ=
m9yLW1vbmV5P2NtcGlkPUJCRDEyMTkyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRt=
X3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMTIxOSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHV=
mZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB124dcc63" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"=
itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none=
 !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border=
-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  may or may not have had a =
solvency crisis</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0the assets might or might not have been =
worth less than the liabilities =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it certainly had a liqui=
dity crisis; customers wanted their money back and there wasn=E2=80=99t eno=
ugh ready money for them. The exchange solved the liquidity crisis, not nec=
essarily by coming up with ready money, but by=C2=A0<em>slowing the withdra=
wals</em>. (By <em>promising </em>to have ready money.)=C2=A0The exchange c=
an say =E2=80=9Ceverything is fine now, we have your money,=E2=80=9D and cu=
stomers will breathe a sigh of relief and stop withdrawing money.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, in the general case, the bankrupt crypto =
lending platform was saying exactly that =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Ceverything=
 is fine, we have your money=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0minutes before freezin=
g withdrawals and filing for bankruptcy. The thing that has changed is that=
 people didn=E2=80=99t believe the bankrupt crypto platform, so they kept w=
ithdrawing money and it collapsed; they believe the big exchange that took =
it over, so they stop.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If they=C2=A0<em>don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>stop =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0if this doesn=E2=80=99t work =E2=80=94=C2=A0then hopefully t=
he big exchange has the money to pay them out! But the big exchange won=E2=
=80=99t, like,=C2=A0put all that money in an escrow account just to win a b=
ankruptcy auction. The big exchange will go to the bankrupt platform=E2=80=
=99s executives and bankruptcy advisers, and the bankruptcy court, and say =
=E2=80=9Ctrust us, we=E2=80=99re good for the money, we=E2=80=99re a big ex=
change.=E2=80=9D Hopefully they will do some due diligence on the exchange =
and satisfy themselves that that=E2=80=99s true. But, in crypto, things cha=
nge fast, and it=E2=80=99s weirdly hard to get audited financial statements=
.</p>

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Crypto prices, and firms, crashed =

=C2=A0<em>already=C2=A0</em>using customer deposits for bad reasons, and lo=
sing them, then acquiring more customer deposits would be a way to keep thi=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Our bid reflects our deeply held belief that c=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">New to Binance.US customers will have access t=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We always maintain 1:1 reserves, and are subje=
ct to regular audits and regulatory reporting by government entities. We do=

orporate debt.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Binance.US is well capitalized: our assets exc=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We will do everything in our power to speed up=

approvals, is to provide users access to their assets in March, 2023.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Presumably Binance.US is sharing those audits =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a public accounting firm, you are i=
n the business of signing your name to a piece of paper saying =E2=80=9Cthi=
s company=E2=80=99s financial statements are basically true,=E2=80=9D and c=
harging for it. Before signing your name, you will do your best to confirm =
that the financial statements are true, but there is always a risk that you=
=E2=80=99re wrong. If you are wrong you might get sued or fined; also, thou=
gh, if you are wrong too often your signature will lose its value and peopl=
e will stop paying you for it. Your high-level goal is to (1) minimize that=
 risk while (2) maximizing your revenue. Broadly speaking, you minimize ris=
k by being conservative, by only auditing the statements of simple trustwor=
thy companies in stable industries, and you maximize revenue by being aggre=
ssive, by auditing the statements of companies in fast-growing industries a=
nd by being, uh, customer-friendly about your audits.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The tensions here are all very obvious and har=
d, but sometimes there are easy calls. Like if you got into aggressively au=
diting statements of companies in a complicated and risky industry because =
you were betting on its rapid growth, and then people lose confidence in th=
e finances of companies in that industry and it starts shrinking, then (1) =
the risk of auditing that industry has gone way up and (2) the potential re=
venue has gone way down. Why bother? <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/30025450.287534/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdz=
L2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTItMTYvbWF6YXJzLXRvLXBhdXNlLWFsbC13b3JrLWZvci1jcnlwdG8=
tY2xpZW50cy1iaW5hbmNlLXNheXMtbGJxYmFtdzc_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTIxOTIyX01PTkVZU1RVRk=
YmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIxMjE5J=
nV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB04b8746c" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
  Anyway</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mazars Group, the accounting firm used by cryp=
to giant Binance Holdings Ltd. and other big players in the industry to vou=
ch for their assets held in reserve, has halted all such work for crypto cl=
ients, dealing a blow to an industry seeking to shore up confidence in the =
wake of FTX=E2=80=99s collapse.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In an email sent by the French firm, Mazars sa=
id it had suspended work for cryptocurrency firms because of indications th=
at markets haven=E2=80=99t been reassured by the =E2=80=9Cproof-of-reserves=
=E2=80=9D reports it had published so far. The firm was also concerned abou=
t intense media scrutiny, the email said. A Mazars spokesperson later said =
the suspension was limited to its provision of proof-of-reserves reports, c=
iting =E2=80=9Cconcerns regarding the way these reports are understood by t=
he public.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Proof-of-reserves reports have faced scrutiny =
as they are not comparable to a full audit, in that they only show a firm=
=E2=80=99s assets, not its liabilites, and instead serve as snapshots in ti=
me that say information provided by clients broadly checks out. =E2=80=A6</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CMany audit firms are scared to work w=
ith crypto businesses,=E2=80=9D said Binance CEO Changpeng =E2=80=9CCZ=E2=
=80=9D Zhao in a Thursday interview on CNBC. When asked why Binance hasn=E2=
=80=99t engaged a Big Four auditor =E2=80=94 a moniker that refers to the l=
argest accountancy companies PwC, Deloitte, EY and KPMG =E2=80=94 Zhao adde=
d that such firms =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t even know how to audit crypto exch=
anges.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In theory, auditing is especially valuable in =
an industry where some big firms are giant scams and some are not and it is=
 hard to tell the difference from the outside. In practice, it is not clear=
 that auditors can tell the difference either, and the risk is high.</p>

=C2=A0stablecoin,=C2=A0making off with $1,075,000 in DAI.</p>
